$comment: Cedar editorial rules. Read-only to automation. All changes require human PR + ADR.
voice: active, single unified Cedar Voice — that of a candid senior engineer — throughout all sections
case: sentence — first word and proper nouns only
prohibited: passive voice where active is possible
source file names as change descriptions
file counts as the substance of a change
generic migration steps without specific token or prop names
starting a bullet with a component or token name — always lead with the action
describing what changed without explaining what it unlocks — 'modular imports are now available' is not enough; say what a team can now do that they could not do before
overview that leads with implementation details before consumer value — architecture first, benefit second is the wrong order
new feature entries that omit a before/after capability comparison — the reader must be able to answer 'why would I care about this?'
required: new feature entries must include a 'to get started' line

new feature entries must include a before/after capability comparison: what a team can do now that they could not do (or could only do with significant friction) before — one concrete example per feature is the minimum
overview must open with consumer value, not implementation detail — the architectural reason for a change belongs after the benefit, never before it
overview must include at least one concrete capability example drawn from release-intent.md 'What consumers will care about most'
improvements and bug-fixes bullets must begin with a past-tense active verb: Fixed, Added, Updated, Removed
deprecation entries must include a planned removal version and a replacement
breaking change entries must include why the change was made
migration steps must name the specific token, prop, or import path
overview must state whether the reader needs to take action

qualityChecks.$comment: unifyDraftTone (Opus) runs these on the complete draft before finalizing.
qualityChecks.humanTest: Would a senior engineer not on this project understand this in one read?
qualityChecks.ticketTest: Does this read like it was copied from an engineering ticket? If yes, rewrite.
qualityChecks.parallelTest: Do all entries in this section follow the same structure?
qualityChecks.overviewTest: Does the overview open with a consumer capability, not an implementation detail? Does it include a concrete example? Does it state whether action is required?
qualityChecks.capabilityTest: For every new-features entry: can the reader answer 'what can I do now that I could not do before?' If no, the before/after contrast is missing — add it.
qualityChecks.architectureLeadTest: Does any sentence in the overview or new-features section lead with an architectural reason before the consumer benefit? If yes, flip the order.
qualityChecks.migrationTest: Is every migration step concrete enough to write a codemod from?
qualityChecks.voiceTest: Does the entire document read as if one person wrote it?
qualityChecks.breakingFormatTest: Every breaking change entry must have bold **Why:** **Before:** **After:** **Migrate:** sub-bullets. Plain 'Why:' without bold is a formatting failure — rewrite.
qualityChecks.fileListTest: Does the output contain any file paths, file counts, or 'Changes in this branch' sections? If yes, strip them entirely before finalizing.

voiceCalibration.$comment: Read this file before generating any human-facing output. It is the canonical standard.
voiceCalibration.purpose: Calibrate tone and structure to the ideal Cedar release standard. Do not deviate from this style.

sectionGuidance.overview: Open with the headline consumer benefit — what teams can now do, not what changed internally. Follow with one concrete capability example. Close with whether action is required and the co-release dependency if one exists. 2–4 sentences total. The architectural reason for any change belongs after the benefit, never before it. Test: if you removed the first sentence, would the reader know what this release is for? If yes, move that sentence to the front.
sectionGuidance.do-i-need-to-update: Two lines maximum. Binary answer with one-line reason.
sectionGuidance.breaking-changes: What changed, why, and how to fix it. Always use specific names.
sectionGuidance.new-features: Each entry has three parts: (1) the headline capability in bold — name what a team can now do; (2) the before/after contrast — one sentence on what this replaces or what friction it removes, with a concrete code example where the diff is non-obvious; (3) a 'To get started' line with the minimum action. The before/after contrast is mandatory. If a feature has no meaningful before state (it is entirely net-new), say so explicitly rather than omitting the contrast.
sectionGuidance.improvements: One concise sentence per item, leading with a past-tense active verb.
sectionGuidance.bug-fixes: What was wrong and what is fixed. Technical and blameless.
sectionGuidance.bolo: Candid about gotchas. What to watch for and why, in plain language.
sectionGuidance.token-changes: Accurate and scannable. Translate token shifts into design impact where relevant.
sectionGuidance.migration-guide: Numbered steps. Active voice. Group batch changes. Before/after code blocks.
